(3) Learning Hexo from Scratch - How to Install Hexo

This post covers installing Hexo on both Windows and Mac. After this one, the rest of the series will mainly use Mac for examples — the only real difference is in the install step, and everything past that is basically the same.

Before installing Hexo, we need to get the dev environment in place. From the previous post, you know Hexo is built on Node.js, so we have to install Node.js first. I'd also recommend installing Git — whether or not Git is installed will affect later steps.

When you go to the Node.js or Git official sites, the install page will auto-detect your OS and show the right download. I won't go into Git install in detail — just feed "Git install" to your search engine of choice. So here I'll only cover installing Node.js.
Stands for Long-Term Support. Basically the current stable version.
For real-world project work, the recommendation is to go with LTS, unless there's some specific new feature you want to try out — then you'd grab Current.

So which Node.js version should you pick? Usually LTS. If you're not sure, this series will use Hexo 4, and as I mentioned last post, the official benchmark shows Hexo 4's speed across different Node.js versions:
LTS right now is 12, so you can just install LTS and you're good.

If you've got a bunch of different Node.js versions on your machine and need to switch between them, I'd recommend the approach in these two posts. I use nvm myself for switching versions, so I'm passing the recommendation along:
Installing nvm on Mac OS X (article in Mandarin)
Installing nvm on Windows 10 to switch Node.js versions (article in Mandarin)
Now open up your terminal. You can search for "Terminal.app" or just "Terminal". I personally use iTerm, so I'll be using that for the demos (it just looks nicer).
Before installing Hexo, I'd recommend running these to confirm Node.js and npm are installed correctly (npm comes bundled with Node.js):
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
If both spit out version numbers, you're good. For our setup, the Node.js version should be the LTS version, 12 — so you should see something like
(npm version doesn't really matter, you just need to confirm it's installed, otherwise you can't install packages).
Then run this to install Hexo:
On Mac you might hit a compile issue during install. The official docs cover this, so I'll paste it directly:
You might hit issues during compile. Install Xcode from the App Store first. Once Xcode is installed, open it and go to
Preferences -> Download -> Command Line Tools -> Install
to install the command line tools.
Usually if you've already installed Git, you won't hit this error.
If you see the info in the image above, congrats — Hexo is installed.

Windows install isn't much different. I'll use PowerShell here as the terminal. You might wonder why my PowerShell looks nicer — that's because I use Windows Terminal, which you can install from the Microsoft Store. If you're into customising your terminal, you can check out my post on this: "
Customising Windows PowerShell (Windows Terminal) (article in Mandarin)
Back on track: once Windows Terminal is open, same habit — first confirm your Node version and npm:
Same as Mac, install Hexo with:
When you see the info above, you're installed.
That's it for today's Ironman post — congrats on getting Hexo installed. See you in the next one
